title: LEARNING TO PROMPT FOR VISION-LANGUAGE MODELS [PDF]

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## LEARNING TO PROMPT FOR VISION-LANGUAGE MODELS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Vision-language pre-training has recently emerged as a promising alternative for representation learning. It shifts from the tradition of using images and discrete labels for learning a fixed set of weights, seen as visual concepts, to aligning images and raw text for two separate encoders. Such a paradigm benefits from a broader source of supervision and allows zero-shot transfer to downstream tasks since visual concepts can be diametrically generated from natural language, known as prompt. In this paper, we identify that a major challenge of deploying such models in practice is prompt engineering. This is because designing a proper prompt, especially for context words surrounding a class name, requires domain expertise and typically takes a significant amount of time for words tuning since a slight change in wording could have a huge impact on performance. Moreover, different downstream tasks require specific designs, further hampering the efficiency of deployment. To overcome this challenge, we propose a simple approach named _context optimization (CoOp)_ . The main idea is to model context in prompts using continuous representations and perform end-to-end learning from data while keeping the pre-trained parameters fixed. In this way, the design of task-relevant prompts can be fully automated. Experiments on 11 datasets show that CoOp effectively turns pre-trained vision-language models into data-efficient visual learners, requiring as few as one or two shots to beat hand-crafted prompts with a decent margin and able to gain significant improvements when using more shots (e.g., at 16 shots the average gain is around 17% with the highest reaching over 50%). CoOp also exhibits strong robustness to distribution shift.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The traditional approach for visual representation learning is to train vision models to predict for a fixed set of object categories using discrete labels (He et al., 2016; Dosovitskiy et al., 2021). However, this approach limits visual recognition systems to closed-set visual concepts defined during training, making them unable to handle new categories once deployed in target environments, since additional data are required for learning a new classifier. Recently, vision-language pre-training such as CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) and ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021) has emerged as a promising alternative. The main idea is to align images and raw text using two separate encoders—one for each modality. Through large-scale pre-training, vision-language models are allowed to learn open-set visual concepts and can readily be transferred to downstream tasks. In particular, for each new classification task, one can synthesize the classification weights by feeding natural language describing classes of interest to the text encoder, and compare them with image features produced by the image encoder.

We observe that for pre-trained vision-language models, the text input, known as prompt, plays a key role in downstream datasets. However, identifying the right prompt is a non-trivial task, which often takes a significant amount of time for words tuning—since a slight change in wording could make a huge difference in performance. For instance, for Caltech101 (Figure 1(a), 2nd vs. 3rd prompt), adding “a” before the class token brings more than 5% increase in accuracy. Moreover, prompt engineering also requires expertise about the task and ideally the language model’s underlying mechanism. This is exemplified in Figure 1(b-d) where adding task-relevant context can lead to significant improvements, i.e., “flower” for Flowers102, “texture” for DTD and “satellite” for EuroSAT. Tuning the sentence structure could bring further improvements, e.g., putting “a type of flower” after the class token for Flowers102, keeping only “texture” in the context for DTD, and

Inspired by recent prompt learning research in NLP (Shin et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2020; Zhong et al., 2021), we propose _context optimization (CoOp)_[1] to automate prompt engineering to allow more efficient and task-specific transfer for pre-trained vision-language models. Specifically, we model a prompt’s context using continuous representations which are essentially initialized with random vectors with the same dimension as word embeddings (see Figure 2). The context could be shared among all classes or designed to be class-specific. During training, we simply minimize the prediction error using the cross-entropy loss with respect to the learnable context vectors, while keeping the pre-trained parameters fixed. The gradients can be back-propagated all the way through the text encoder, distilling the rich knowledge encoded in the parameters for learning task-relevant context.

To demonstrate the effectiveness of CoOp, we benchmark on 11 datasets, which cover a diverse set of visual recognition tasks including classification on generic objects, scenes, actions and fine-grained categories, as well as specialized tasks like recognizing textures and satellite imagery. The results show that CoOp can effectively turn pre-trained vision-language models into data-efficient visual learners, requiring as few as one or two shots to beat hand-crafted prompts with a decent margin. The performance can also be further boosted by using more shots, e.g., at 16 shots the margin over hand-crafted prompts averages at around 17% and reaches over 50% for the highest. CoOp also outperforms the linear probe alternative known as a strong few-shot learning baseline (Tian et al., 2020), and crucially, demonstrates much stronger robustness to distribution shift. Extensive analysis is also conducted to offer a comprehensive picture on how to apply CoOp in practice. The source code for reproducing the experiments will be released to facilitate future research.

## 2 METHODOLOGY

2.1 VISION-LANGUAGE PRE-TRAINING

We briefly introduce vision-language pre-training with a particular focus on CLIP (Radford et al., 2021). Our approach is applicable to broader CLIP-like vision-language models.

**Models** CLIP consists of two encoders, one for images and the other for text. The image encoder aims to map high-dimensional images into a low-dimensional embedding space. The architecture of the image encoder can take the form of a CNN like ResNet-50 (He et al., 2016) or a ViT (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021). On the other hand, the text encoder is built on top of a Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) and aims to generate text representations from natural language.

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

## 3.1 FEW-SHOT LEARNING

**Datasets** We select 11 publicly available image classification datasets used in CLIP: ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009), Caltech101 (Fei-Fei et al., 2004), OxfordPets (Parkhi et al., 2012), StanfordCars (Krause et al., 2013), Flowers102 (Nilsback & Zisserman, 2008), Food101 (Bossard et al., 2014), FGVCAircraft (Maji et al., 2013), SUN397 (Xiao et al., 2010), DTD (Cimpoi et al., 2014), EuroSAT (Helber et al., 2019) and UCF101 (Soomro et al., 2012) (see Appendix A for their statistics). These datasets constitute a comprehensive benchmark, which covers a diverse set of vision tasks including classification on generic objects, scenes, actions and fine-grained categories, as well as specialized tasks like recognizing textures and satellite imagery. We follow the few-shot evaluation protocol adopted in CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), using 1, 2, 4, 8 and 16 shots for training respectively and deploying models in the full test sets. The average results over three runs are reported for comparison.

**Training Details** CoOp has four versions: positioning the class token in the end or middle; unified context vs. CSC. Unless otherwise stated, ResNet-50 (He et al., 2016) is used as the image encoder’s backbone and the number of context tokens _M_ is set to 16. Investigations on other design choices are discussed in Section 3.3. All models are built on top of the open-sourced CLIP.[2] CoOp’s context vectors are randomly initialized by drawing from a zero-mean Gaussian distribution with standard deviation equal to 0.02. Training is done with SGD and an initial learning rate of 0.002, which is decayed by the cosine annealing rule. The maximum epoch is set to 200 for 16/8 shots, 100 for 4/2 shots, and 50 for 1 shot (except for ImageNet where the maximum epoch is fixed to 50). To mitigate explosive gradients observed in the early training iterations, we use the warmup trick by fixing the learning rate to 1 _e−_ 5 during the first epoch.

Overall, CoOp effectively turns CLIP into a strong few-shot learner (solid lines), achieving significant improvements over zero-shot CLIP (stars) and performing favorably against the linear probe alternative (dashed lines). _M_ denotes the context length. “end” or “mid” means putting the class token in the end or middle. CSC means class-specific context.

**Baseline Methods** We compare CoOp with two baseline methods. The first is zero-shot CLIP, which is based on hand-crafted prompts. We follow the guideline of prompt engineering introduced by Radford et al. (2021). For generic objects and scenes, “a photo of a [CLASS].” is adopted. For fine-grained categories, task-relevant context is added like “a type of pet” for OxfordPets and “a type of food” for Food101. When it comes to specialized tasks such as recognizing textures in DTD, the prompt is customized as “[CLASS] texture.” where the class names are adjectives like “bubbly” and “dotted”. See Appendix A for the details. The second baseline is linear probe CLIP. As suggested by Radford et al. (2021) and a recent study on few-shot learning (Tian et al., 2020), training a linear classifier on top of high-quality pre-trained models’ features (like CLIP) can easily achieve performance that is on a par with that of state-of-the-art few-shot learning methods, which are often much more sophisticated. We follow the same training method used by Radford et al. (2021) to train linear probe CLIP.

**Comparison with Hand-Crafted Prompts** Figure 3 summarizes the results. Our default model is CLIP+CoOp with the class token positioned in the end. The two different ways of positioning the class token achieve similar performance as their curves highly overlap. From the average performance displayed in the top-left corner, we observe that CLIP+CoOp is a strong few-shot learner, requiring only two shots on average to obtain a decent margin over zero-shot CLIP. Given 16 shots for training, the average gap brought by CoOp can be further increased to around 17%.

Figure 4 ranks the absolute improvements obtained by CoOp at 16 shots over hand-crafted prompts. Huge improvements are observed on specialized tasks namely EuroSAT and DTD where the increase in performance reaches over 50% and 20% respectively. The jumps in performance are also significant (those more than 10%) on most fine-grained datasets including Flowers102, StanfordCars and FGVCAircraft, as well as on scene and action recognition datasets (SUN397 & UCF101). Since ImageNet is a challenging dataset that contains 1,000 classes, the 5.05% improvement is also noteworthy. In contrast, the increases on the two fine-grained datasets, OxfordPets and Food101, are less appealing. By digging into CLIP+CoOp’s curves on these two datasets in Figure 3, we find there is a loss of momentum in performance improvements even with more shots used, seemingly an overfitting problem. A potential solution is to impose higher regularizations like increasing the weight decay. Nonetheless, the overall results are strong enough to serve as evidence of CoOp’s capability of learning task-relevant prompts in a data-efficient way.

**Comparison with Linear Probe CLIP** In terms of the overall performance (Figure 3, topleft), CLIP+CoOp demonstrates clear advantages over linear probe CLIP. The latter requires 4 shots on average to match the zero-shot’s performance while CoOp’s average gains at 4 shots are already more than 10%. It is also clear that the gaps in the extreme low-data regime such as one or two shots are much larger, suggesting that CoOp is much more effective than learning a linear classifier from scratch for fewshot learning. We also observe that linear probe CLIP is comparable to CLIP+CoOp on the two specialized tasks (DTD & EuroSAT) as well as on a couple of fine-grained datasets (Flowers102 & FGVCAircraft)—this is not too surprising as the pre-trained CLIP space has been

**Unified vs. Class-Specific Context** On average, using unified context leads to better performance. In terms of when to apply CSC and when not to, we have the following suggestions. For generic objects (ImageNet & Caltech101), scenes (SUN397) and actions (UCF101), using unified context is clearly better. Unified context also works better on some fine-grained datasets including OxfordPets and Food101, but on others like StanfordCars, Flowers102 and FGVCAircraft the CSC version is preferred. CSC also yields better performance on the two specialized tasks, DTD and EuroSAT, at 16 shots in particular. However, CSC mostly underperforms unified context in challenging low-data scenarios (fewer than 8 shots), which makes sense because CSC has more parameters than unified context and needs more data for training.

## 3.2 ROBUSTNESS TO DISTRIBUTION SHIFT

Since CoOp requires training on a specific data distribution, it risks learning spurious correlations that are detrimental to generalization in unseen distributions (domains), as suggested in recent studies (Taori et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2021). On the contrary, zero-shot CLIP is not tied to a specific data distribution and has exhibited strong robustness to distribution shift (Radford et al., 2021). In this section, we aim to unveil how robust CoOp is to distribution shift, in comparison to zero-shot CLIP and the linear probe model.

**Datasets** The source dataset is ImageNet. The target datasets are ImageNetV2 (Recht et al., 2019), ImageNet-Sketch (Wang et al., 2019), ImageNet-A (Hendrycks et al., 2021b) and ImageNetR (Hendrycks et al., 2021a), all of which have compatible class names with ImageNet allowing seamless transfer for the prompts learned by CoOp. ImageNetV2 is a reproduced test set using different sources while following ImageNet’s data collection process. ImageNet-Sketch contains sketch images belonging to the same 1,000 ImageNet classes. Both ImageNet-A and -R contain 200 classes derived from a subset of ImageNet’s 1,000 classes. The former consists of real-world adversarially filtered images that cause current ImageNet classifiers to produce low results, whereas the latter features a rendition of the ImageNet classes in diverse image styles such as paintings, cartoons and sculptures.

**Results** Table 1 summarizes the results. It is surprising that CLIP+CoOp exhibits stronger robustness than zero-shot CLIP to distribution shift, despite exposure to the source dataset. This suggests that the learned prompts are also generalizable. Moreover, it is interesting to see that using fewer context tokens leads to better robustness. More results with different vision backbones are provided in Appendix B.1 where the conclusion remains the same. In contrast, linear probe CLIP obtains much worse results on these target datasets, exposing its weakness in domain generalization.

## 3.3 FURTHER ANALYSIS

**Comparison with Prompt Ensembling** Radford et al. (2021) have suggested that additional improvements can be obtained by ensembling over multiple zero-shot classifiers generated using different hand-crafted prompts, such as “a photo of the large [CLASS].”, “a bad photo of the [CLASS].” and “a origami [CLASS].”, which reflect a different scale, view and abstraction respectively for an image. We are interested to know whether the prompts learned by CoOp can still maintain advantages when compared with prompt ensembling. For fair comparison, we use the select prompts from Radford et al. (2021), which have been extensively tuned on ImageNet, to construct the ensemble classifier. Table 2 presents the results of prompt engineering (i.e., using a single hand-crafted prompt), prompt ensembling and CoOp, confirming that CoOp is still the best performing method. Additional results are provided in Appendix B.2 to show that CoOp also beats prompt ensembling when more advanced vision backbones are used. Given the potential of prompt ensembling, future work could investigate how to improve CoOp from the ensembling perspective.

**Context Length** How many context tokens should be used? And is it better to have more context tokens? The results in Section 3.2 suggest having shorter context length benefits domain generalization. Here we study this hyperparameter for source datasets. Specifically, we repeat experiments on the 11 datasets by varying the context length from 4 to 8 to 16. The average results are shown in Figure 5(a), which indicate that having more context tokens leads to better performance and that positioning the class token in the middle gains more momentum with longer context length. **Vision Backbones** Figure 5(b) summarizes the results on the 11 datasets using a variety of vision backbones covering both CNNs and ViTs. The results are expected: the more advanced the backbone, the better the performance. The gap between CoOp and hand-crafted prompts is significant across all architectures. **Initialization** We compare random initialization with manual initialization. The latter uses the embeddings of “a photo of a” to initialize the context vectors for the 11 datasets. For fair comparison, we also set the context length to 4 when using random initialization. Table 3 suggests a “good” initialization only brings a small improvement. Though further tuning of the initialization words might help, in practice we suggest using the simple random initialization method.

**Interpreting the Learned Prompts** is difficult because the context vectors are optimized in a continuous space. We resort to an indirect way by searching within the vocabulary for words that are closest to the learned vectors based on the Euclidean distance. Note that CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) uses the BPE representation (Sennrich et al., 2016) for tokenization, so the vocabulary includes subwords that frequently appear in text, such as “hu” (subsumed by many words like “hug” and “human”). Table 4 shows the searched results on some datasets. We observe that a few words are somewhat relevant to the tasks, such as “enjoyed” for Food101, “fluffy” and “paw” for OxfordPets, and “pretty” for DTD. But when connecting all the nearest words together, the prompts do not make much sense. We also observe that when using manual initialization (like “a photo of a”), the nearest words for the converged vectors are mostly the ones used for initialization. We conjecture that the learned vectors might encode meanings that are beyond the existing vocabulary. Overall, we are unable to draw any firm conclusion based on the observations because using nearest words to interpret the learned prompts could be inaccurate—the semantics of the vectors is not necessarily correlated with the nearest words.

## 4 RELATED WORK

**Vision-Language Models** have recently demonstrated great potential in learning generic visual representations and allowing zero-shot transfer to a variety of downstream classification tasks (Radford et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020). To our knowledge, the recent developments in vision-language learning, particularly CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) and ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), are largely driven by advances in the following three areas: i) text representation learning with Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017), ii) large-minibatch contrastive representation learning (Chen et al., 2020; He et al., 2020; H´enaff et al., 2020), and iii) web-scale training datasets—CLIP benefits from 400 million curated image-text pairs while ALIGN exploits 1.8 billion noisy image-text pairs.
The idea of mapping images and text onto a common embedding space has been studied since nearly a decade ago (Socher et al., 2013; Frome et al., 2013; Elhoseiny et al., 2013), but with drastically

different technologies. For text features extraction, early work has mainly utilized pre-trained word vectors (Socher et al., 2013; Frome et al., 2013) or the hand-crafted TF-IDF features (Elhoseiny et al., 2013; Lei Ba et al., 2015). Matching images and text features has been formulated as metric learning (Frome et al., 2013), multi-label classification (Joulin et al., 2016; Gomez et al., 2017), n-gram language learning (Li et al., 2017), and the recently proposed captioning (Desai & Johnson, 2021). Our work is orthogonal to recent research in vision-language models, aiming to facilitate the deployment of such models in downstream datasets.

**Prompt Learning in NLP** Knowledge probing for large pre-trained language models, formally defined by Petroni et al. (2019) as “fill-in-the-blank” cloze tests, has recently sparked interest in prompt learning research in NLP (Shin et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2020; Li & Liang, 2021; Zhong et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021b). The basic idea of knowledge probing is to induce pre-trained language models to generate answers given cloze-style prompts, which can benefit a number of downstream tasks, such as sentiment analysis. Jiang et al. (2020) propose to generate candidate prompts through text mining and paraphrasing, and identify the optimal ones that give the highest training accuracy. Shin et al. (2020) introduce a gradient-based approach, which searches for tokens with the largest gradient changes in the label likelihood. Most related to our work are continuous prompt learning methods (Zhong et al., 2021; Li & Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021) which optimize continuous vectors in the word embedding space. A drawback of such methods compared to searching discrete tokens is the lack of a clear way to visualize what “words” are learned for the vectors. We refer readers to Liu et al. (2021a) for a comprehensive survey in the topic of prompt learning in NLP.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We present CoOp, a differentiable approach that focuses on continuous prompt learning to facilitate the deployment of pre-trained vision-language models in downstream datasets. The results on the 11 datasets serve as strong evidence of CoOp’s effectiveness in data-efficient learning. The learned prompts are proved much more task-relevant than hand-crafted prompts reflected by the huge improvements in performance, as well as stronger in robustness to distribution shift. In terms of limitation, CoOp requires explicit access to the pre-trained model parameters, which might be unavailable when only the APIs of pre-trained models are provided. An interesting future direction is thus to investigate “black-box” prompt learning.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a critical bottleneck in deploying vision-language models like CLIP: the sensitivity and manual labor associated with prompt engineering. The proposed method, Context Optimization (CoOp), is a high-quality contribution that effectively transitions from discrete, manual prompt tuning to a continuous, learnable representation approach. The clarity of the motivation is excellent; the authors provide compelling evidence via Figure 1 that even minor linguistic variations significantly impact zero-shot performance. The methodology is technically sound, leveraging backpropagation through the text encoder to optimize "soft" prompt tokens while keeping the backbone frozen, which is both parameter-efficient and computationally sensible. The most significant strength is the extensive empirical validation across 11 diverse datasets, demonstrating substantial gains over hand-crafted prompts in few-shot scenarios. However, the primary issues to address in the full review would be the potential for overfitting the continuous embeddings in extremely low-data regimes and a more detailed comparison of the computational overhead of CoOp versus traditional linear probing. Overall, this is a top-tier submission that provides a scalable solution to a timely problem in the vision-language community.

CLIP's official linear probe results typically use specific solvers (like Scikit-Learn's LogisticRegression with L2 regularization). If the authors used standard SGD for a low-shot setting, it would explain the poor baseline performance. Without clarifying if an optimized few-shot solver was used, the claim of superiority over linear probing may be an artifact of the training setup rather than the method itself.

comments.title: Potential Overclaim Regarding Robustness to Distribution Shift
comments.quote: It is surprising that CLIP+CoOp exhibits stronger robustness than zero-shot CLIP to distribution shift, despite exposure to the source dataset.
comments.explanation: Robustness is typically measured by the performance gap between source and target distributions. While CoOp shows higher absolute accuracy on target datasets (ImageNetV2, etc.), it also shows a proportional increase in source accuracy (ImageNet). This suggests that CoOp is inheriting the pre-existing robustness of the CLIP backbone rather than enhancing it. The claim that it 'exhibits stronger robustness' may misinterpret absolute performance gains for gains in distributional resilience.

Furthermore, Table 1 shows that $M=4$ outperforms $M=16$ on the 'Source' (ImageNet) set. This is counter-intuitive as more parameters ($M=16$) should lead to better fitting on the source data. This suggests potential under-training or optimization issues for the $M=16$ model that are not addressed in the text.

comments.quote: We resort to an indirect way by searching within the vocabulary for words that are closest to the learned vectors based on the Euclidean distance.
comments.explanation: The authors use Euclidean distance to interpret learned vectors, but CLIP is trained using cosine similarity. In high-dimensional spaces trained on spherical contrastive losses, Euclidean distance can be misleading as magnitudes may be dominated by frequency priors rather than semantic similarity. Cosine similarity would be the technically consistent metric for identifying 'nearest neighbor' words in the CLIP embedding space.

A score of 55.41% is significantly lower than the standard baseline for this specific architecture. While the internal comparison between CoOp and the authors' own baseline is consistent, the baseline performance itself seems low for the stated backbone (ResNet-50), which might slightly exaggerate the relative gain. Looking at the Target datasets (ImageNetV2, Sketch, A, R), the best results are consistently achieved by M=4. However, the 'Source' performance for M=16 (60.46) is actually lower than M=8 and M=4 on the source distribution itself. This is counter-intuitive if more parameters (longer context) usually lead to better fitting on the source. While it doesn't invalidate the robustness claim (M=4 is indeed robust), it suggests that the M=16 model might have been slightly under-trained or over-fitted compared to the smaller M values on the source set, which isn't addressed in the text.

comments.explanation: The text compares CoOp and Linear Probe CLIP at 4 shots. However, a key methodological detail is omitted: standard 'Linear Probe' evaluations in CLIP (Radford et al. 2021) often use the full dataset. The authors state they follow the training method from Radford et al., but in a few-shot context, the performance of a linear probe is highly sensitive to the regularization (e.g., Logistic Regression with L2 vs. a simple Linear Layer with SGD). If the Linear Probe is trained with standard SGD for only a few iterations on 1-4 shots, it is expected to perform poorly compared to a prompt-tuning method. Without specifying if they used an optimized few-shot solver (like the scikit-learn LogisticRegression used by Radford), the comparison '4 shots on average to match zero-shot' might be an artifact of the specific linear probe implementation rather than an inherent limitation of training a linear head.

For this to represent cosine similarity consistently with the CLIP pre-training objective, $f$ and $w_i$ must be explicitly normalized to unit length. If they are not, the magnitude of the vectors would interfere with the temperature parameter $\tau$, potentially leading to optimization instability during CoOp training. progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The proposed paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound approach to a timely problem in vision-language pre-training: the sensitivity and inefficiency of hand-crafted prompts. The writing is clear and logically structured, effectively transitioning from the limitations of manual prompt engineering to the elegance of "context optimization" (CoOp). The paper’s greatest strength lies in its strong empirical validation; the performance gains across 11 datasets—particularly in low-data regimes and under distribution shift—provide compelling evidence for the practical utility of the method. The most significant issue to address in subsequent sections is the potential overhead of CoOp; while the authors emphasize efficiency through fixed-parameter training, a more detailed discussion of the computational cost of optimizing these continuous prompts versus standard linear probing would strengthen the argument for its deployment in real-world scenarios. Overall, the work is highly readable and posits a foundational shift in how we interface with frozen foundational models.

Specifically: the prompt structure, the class-specific context (CSC) constraint—which is currently written as a logically vacuous equality—and the prediction probability function are all technically underspecified. They characterise higher absolute accuracy on shifted datasets as 'stronger robustness' without accounting for the fact that CoOp is fine-tuned on target-class-aligned data, unlike zero-shot CLIP. Additionally, the claim that linear probes require 4 shots to match zero-shot is contradicted by the visual data in Figure 3, and the 'average' improvement claims are mathematically opaque given the high variance across datasets.

It is standard for CSC to imply distinct learnable parameters per class, but the OCR makes it appear as an equality constraint for $i=j$, which is trivial. As this is the formal definition of a key variant of their method, the inability to parse the intended mathematical definition is a technical hinderance.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of the prediction probability function
comments.quote:
The prediction probability is computed as

where the class token within each prompt $t_i$ is replaced by the corresponding word embedding vector(s) of the $i$-th class name.
comments.explanation: Similar to the prompt form, the equation defining the prediction probability (likely a Softmax over the logits) is missing after the colon. The text describes the procedure, but the lack of the formal equation (which usually involves the temperature parameter $\tau$ mentioned elsewhere) prevents a precise understanding of the normalization applied to the class scores. This makes the method's implementation details slightly underspecified.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding linear probe CLIP performance
comments.quote: The latter requires 4 shots on average to match the zero-shot’s performance while CoOp’s average gains at 4 shots are already more than 10%.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, the authors compare CoOp to linear probe CLIP. They state that the linear probe requires 4 shots to match the zero-shot performance. Looking at Figure 3 (top-left), the zero-shot performance (stars) is approx 65%, while linear probe (dashed line) at 4 shots is around 68-70%. While the linear probe does outperform zero-shot at 4 shots, the phrasing implies a threshold behavior that isn't clearly supported by the curve, as the linear probe exceeds zero-shot performance much earlier (between 1-2 shots). This overstates the advantage of CoOp in the extremely low-shot regime compared to the linear baseline.

comments.title: Inconsistent reporting of improvement values
comments.quote: at 16 shots the average gain is around 17% with the highest reaching over 50%
comments.explanation: The abstract states the highest gain reaches over 50% at 16 shots. Figure 4 shows EuroSAT improvement at 50.07%. While this is 'over 50%', Figure 4 also lists the improvement for DTD as 21.83% and others. The text in the introduction claims: 'at 16 shots the margin over hand-crafted prompts averages at around 17% and reaches over 50% for the highest.' This is a specific claim, but checking the dataset list and Figure 4, the interpretation of 'average' gain is opaque, as it isn't explicitly calculated as an arithmetic mean of the 11 datasets presented in Figure 4. The claim is qualitatively plausible, but mathematically underspecified given the high variance in gains (from 1.76% to 50.07%).

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'linear probe' training procedure
comments.quote: We follow the same training method used by Radford et al. (2021) to train linear probe CLIP.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in class-specific context definition
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, the authors define class-specific context (CSC). The text says: 'where context vectors are independent to each class, i.e., [V]1 [i] [V]2 [i] . . . [V]M [i] = [V]1 [j] [V]2 [j] . . . [V]M [j] for i = j and i, j ∈ {1, . . . , K}.' Mathematically, for context to be 'class-specific', one would expect the vectors to be *different* when i ≠ j. The current phrasing using the equality 'for i = j' is a self-evident tautology that implies nothing about the i ≠ j case. Given the context (CSC variant), the authors clearly intend to state that the vectors are allowed to vary for i ≠ j, but the current formal definition is logically vacuous.

comments.title: Potential misstatement regarding CLIP's zero-shot robustness
comments.quote: It is surprising that CLIP+CoOp exhibits stronger robustness than zero-shot CLIP to distribution shift, despite exposure to the source dataset.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that CoOp, which is trained (fine-tuned) on the source dataset, is *more* robust to distribution shift than the zero-shot CLIP baseline. In the 'Results' paragraph of Section 3.2, they suggest this indicates the prompts are 'generalizable'. However, standard literature on distribution shift (e.g., Domain Generalization research) typically views training on a single source distribution as a risk for picking up spurious correlations that *decrease* robustness. While the empirical result might hold, citing it as 'surprising' is correct, but the claim that this proves 'generalizable' prompts is an over-interpretation. It is more likely that the optimized context simply shifts the feature alignment to a region of the embedding space that happens to be more invariant, but framing this as 'stronger robustness than zero-shot' (which is the gold standard for distribution invariance) requires more cautious language to avoid overclaiming.

comments.title: OCR / Transcription error in CSC definition
comments.explanation: The OCR extraction of the mathematical definition for Class-Specific Context (CSC) is incoherent, containing garbled bracket and operator notation. While the surrounding text 'context vectors are independent to each class' and the earlier logical description clarify the intent (i.e., [V]_i^m != [V]_j^m for i != j), the provided mathematical representation is unusable as written. This is a technical issue stemming from OCR failure that prevents the reader from formalizing the precise notation used for CSC.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for weight vector index
comments.explanation: The notation for the set of weight vectors is rendered as '{ w_i} [K] i =1', which is garbled. While standard set-builder notation (e.g., {w_i}_{i=1}^K) is implied and clear in context, the specific formatting error in the OCR makes the formal indexing ambiguous for a reader attempting to implement the model based on the text alone. Given the surrounding clear context, this is a minor technical issue.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in Equation (2) reference
comments.quote: where the class token within each prompt **t**_i is replaced by the corresponding word embedding vector(s) of the _i_-th class name.
comments.explanation: The prompt definition in Section 2.2 defines the prompt structure via variable 't' (a sequence of vectors). The text then refers to 't_i', which is not explicitly defined in the previous formula (Equation 2). While it is clear from context that 't_i' refers to the prompt instance corresponding to class 'i', the transition from a unified 't' to class-specific 't_i' without formalizing the index 'i' in the prompt definition is a minor oversight that could briefly confuse an implementer.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript presents a well-motivated and timely contribution by introducing Context Optimization (CoOp), which automates prompt design for frozen vision-language models via learnable continuous context vectors. The motivation is clearly articulated, effectively capturing the brittleness and expertise dependency of manual prompt engineering, and the core idea is intuitively sound and aligns with recent soft-prompt learning paradigms. The writing is generally lucid, logically structured, and the empirical claims regarding few-shot data efficiency, cross-dataset generalization, and distribution shift robustness are compelling. However, several high-level issues must be addressed to meet publication standards. First, the paper’s novelty and positioning relative to concurrent prompt-tuning and context-optimization methods are underdeveloped; a rigorous comparison with linear probing, manual prompt ensembling, and contemporary soft-prompt techniques is essential to validate the claimed advances. Second, while framed as computationally lightweight, the manuscript lacks early discussion on context-length sensitivity, gradient propagation dynamics through the frozen text encoder, and potential overfitting risks under extreme low-shot regimes. Finally, the strong claims regarding few-shot performance and robustness would benefit from tighter experimental controls (e.g., explicit augmentation protocols, data-leakage verification) and more systematic ablations explaining why continuous contexts effectively distill frozen multimodal representations. Resolving these points would substantially strengthen the paper’s technical rigor, reproducibility, and community impact.

comments.title: Misused word in abstract
comments.quote: visual concepts can be diametrically generated from natural language, known as prompt.
comments.explanation: The authors use 'diametrically', which typically means completely opposite or directly contrary, contradicting the intended meaning. Given the context of zero-shot transfer and vision-language alignment, this is likely a typo for 'dynamically' or 'automatically'. The technical claim is sound, but the word choice is confusing and should be corrected to match standard field phrasing.

comments.title: Non-standard terminology for prompt optimization
comments.quote: typically takes a significant amount of time for words tuning since a slight change in wording could have a huge impact on performance.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'words tuning' is not standard in vision-language or prompt learning literature. The authors correctly use 'prompt tuning', 'prompt learning', and 'text tuning' elsewhere. Using established terms like 'prompt tuning' or 'word tuning' would improve precision and consistency with prior work (e.g., Shin et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2022).

comments.title: Grammatical and phrasing issues in methodology description
comments.quote: Training CLIP is trained to align the two embedding spaces learned for images and text respectively. ... CLIP maximizes the cosine similarity for matched pairs while minimizes the cosine similarity for all other unmatched pairs. ... minimizes the co-sine similarity for all other unmatched pairs.
comments.explanation: Several drafting and editing oversights appear in Section 2.1. First, 'Training CLIP is trained to align...' is redundantly repetitive; it should be streamlined to 'CLIP is trained to align...'. Second, the parallel structure breaks in 'maximizes... while minimizes', which should read 'maximizes... while minimizing...'. Third, 'co-sine similarity' is a typographical error for 'cosine similarity'. While these do not affect mathematical correctness, they hinder readability and professional polish.

comments.title: Undefined notation and inconsistent indexing for Class-Specific Context (CSC)
comments.quote: Another option is to design class-specific context (CSC) where context vectors are independent to each class, i.e., [V]1 _[i]_[[][V][]] _[i]_ 2 _[. . .]_[ [][V][]] _[i] M_[= [][V][]] _[j]_ 1[[][V][]] _[j]_ 2 _[. . .]_[ [][V][]] _[j] M_[for] _[ i]_[=] _[j]_[and] _[ i, j][∈{]_[1] _[, . . . , K][}]_.
comments.explanation: The text introduces CSC notation `[V]_m^i` (and `j`) for class-specific vectors without prior formal definition. Earlier, unified context uses subscript notation `[V]_m`. The sudden shift to superscripts for class indexing contradicts established notation and is not explained, requiring readers to guess that `i` indexes the class. While the prose clarifies the intent, the formal gap complicates precise mathematical specification and reproduction.

comments.title: Variable mismatch and missing probability formula in Section 2.2
comments.quote:
By forwarding a prompt _**t**_ to the text encoder _g_ ( _·_ ), we can obtain a classification weight vector representing a visual concept. The prediction probability is computed as

where the class token within each prompt _**t** i_ is replaced by the corresponding word embedding vector(s) of the _i_ -th class name.
comments.explanation: The text introduces a single prompt `t`, then abruptly switches to indexed prompts `t_i` when describing class token replacement, without clarifying the relationship (e.g., that CoOp uses `K` class-specific prompts). Additionally, the explicit probability formula is missing between 'computed as' and 'where...', and the surrounding text focuses on prompt construction rather than the softmax over class scores. This underspecifies the mathematical pipeline for few-shot classification.

comments.title: Overstated and oversimplified claims regarding robustness and generalization
comments.quote: It is surprising that CLIP+CoOp exhibits stronger robustness than zero-shot CLIP to distribution shift, despite exposure to the source dataset. This suggests that the learned prompts are also generalizable.
comments.explanation: The authors claim CoOp consistently outperforms zero-shot CLIP on distribution shifts, which overgeneralizes empirical results. CoOp is known to degrade significantly on adversarial/real-world shifts (e.g., ImageNet-A, ImageNet-R) compared to zero-shot CLIP. Furthermore, attributing this advantage solely to 'prompt generalizability' oversimplifies the mechanism, ignoring factors like implicit regularization, better text feature space alignment, and reduced modality mismatch. The claim should be qualified by specific benchmarks, and the discussion should acknowledge the trade-off between few-shot source performance and robustness to severe domain shifts.

comments.title: Unqualified generalization about context length and robustness
comments.quote: Moreover, it is interesting to see that using fewer context tokens leads to better robustness.
comments.explanation: This statement lacks necessary conditionality. The relationship between context length $M$ and robustness is dataset-dependent: smaller $M$ can act as a regularizer and generalize better on certain shifts by avoiding source overfitting, but frequently underperforms larger $M$ on others. Without specifying which targets exhibit this trend, readers might incorrectly assume reducing $M$ universally improves robustness. The authors should clarify the scope or frame it as a capacity/regularization effect.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of "momentum" in hyperparameter analysis
comments.quote: The average results are shown in Figure 5(a), which indicate that having more context tokens leads to better performance and that positioning the class token in the middle gains more momentum with longer context length.
comments.explanation: The authors use 'momentum' to describe the performance advantage of the middle-class-token variant as context length increases. In optimization, 'momentum' refers to a specific acceleration term in gradient descent. Here, it is used informally to mean 'performance gain' or 'improvement margin' without clarification, risking misinterpretation. Replacing it with 'performance advantage' or 'margin' would remove ambiguity.

comments.title: Suspicious citation for TF-IDF features
comments.quote: For text features extraction, early work has mainly utilized pre-trained word vectors (Socher et al., 2013; Frome et al., 2013) or the hand-crafted TF-IDF features (Elhoseiny et al., 2013; Lei Ba et al., 2015).
comments.explanation: TF-IDF is a classical representation method predating deep learning-era citations. Citing 'Lei Ba et al., 2015' alongside Elhoseiny et al. (2013) for hand-crafted TF-IDF is historically anachronistic and likely a citation error or mismatched reference. This could mislead readers seeking foundational references on TF-IDF or early multimodal pipelines. The citation should be verified or replaced with an appropriate historical reference.

comments.title: Minor grammatical and typographical errors
comments.quote: such as “a photo of the large [CLASS].”, “a bad photo of the [CLASS].” and “a origami [CLASS].” ... For fair comparison, we use the select prompts from Radford et al. (2021)... Table 4... "fuffy" (1.6099)
comments.explanation: Several minor language and extraction artifacts persist: 'a origami' should be 'an origami'; 'use the select prompts' should be 'the selected prompts'; and Table 4 lists 'fuffy' instead of 'fluffy' (likely an OCR/BPE artifact). While technically harmless, these detract from manuscript professionalism and may cause momentary confusion during reading.

comments.title: Redundant phrasing in methodology description
comments.quote: Training CLIP is trained to align the two embedding spaces learned for images and text respectively.

The authors correctly summarize CLIP's contrastive pre-training objective, but the phrasing is awkward. It should be streamlined to 'CLIP is trained to align...' or 'During training, CLIP aligns...' for professional polish.

comments.title: Subject-verb agreement and parallel structure error
comments.quote: CLIP maximizes the cosine similarity for matched pairs while minimizes the cosine similarity for all other unmatched pairs.
comments.explanation: The verb 'minimizes' breaks parallel structure with 'maximizes'. In academic writing, this clause should read 'CLIP maximizes... while minimizing...' or 'CLIP maximizes... and minimizes...'. This is a minor grammatical issue that does not affect the mathematical correctness of the contrastive loss description, but it slightly hinders readability.

comments.title: Undefined notation and inconsistent indexing for Class-Specific Context (CSC)
comments.quote: Another option is to design class-specific context (CSC) where context vectors are independent to each class, i.e., [V]1 _[i]_[[][V][]] _[i]_ 2 _[. . .]_[ [][V][]] _[i] M_[= [][V][]] _[j]_ 1[[][V][]] _[j]_ 2 _[. . .]_[ [][V][]] _[j] M_[for] _[ i]_[=] _[j]_[and] _[ i, j][∈{]_[1] _[, . . . , K][}]_.
comments.explanation: The provided text contains a heavily garbled OCR rendering of the CSC definition. Even assuming the most plausible intended symbols (e.g., `[V]_1^i [V]_2^i \dots [V]_M^i \neq [V]_1^j [V]_2^j \dots [V]_M^j` for `i \neq j`), the notation `[V]_m^i` is introduced without prior definition. Earlier in Section 2.2, unified context is defined using the subscript `[V]_m`. The sudden use of superscript `i` (and `j`) to denote class-specific parameters is not formally defined, creating a notation inconsistency. A careful reader would need to guess that `i` indexes the class, which contradicts the established `[V]_m` notation. This is a real gap that complicates precise reproduction, though the prose 'context vectors are independent to each class' clarifies the intent.

comments.title: Variable mismatch and missing probability formula in Section 2.2
comments.quote:
By forwarding a prompt _**t**_ to the text encoder _g_ ( _·_ ), we can obtain a classification weight vector representing a visual concept. The prediction probability is computed as

where the class token within each prompt _**t** i_ is replaced by the corresponding word embedding vector(s) of the _i_ -th class name.
comments.explanation: The authors first introduce a single prompt `t`, then immediately switch to `t_i` when describing how the class token is replaced. In few-shot classification with CoOp, there are `K` prompts (one per class), typically denoted `t_i` or `{t_c}_{c=1}^K`. The text conflates the singular `t` with the indexed `t_i` without clarifying the relationship, which is a logical inconsistency in variable usage. Additionally, the probability formula itself is missing between 'computed as' and 'where the class token...', likely due to PDF extraction, but the subsequent text describes how `t_i` is constructed rather than stating the final softmax probability over class scores. While the prose correctly describes prompt construction for CoOp, the absence of the explicit probability equation and the undefined jump from `t` to `t_i` leaves the mathematical pipeline underspecified.

comments.title: Redundant phrasing in Section 2.1
comments.quote: Training CLIP is trained to align the two embedding spaces learned for images and text respectively.
comments.explanation: This sentence contains a clear grammatical redundancy ('Training CLIP is trained to...'). While not a technical error, it reflects sloppy editing that slightly detracts from clarity. Given the context, the intended meaning is unambiguous (CLIP's pre-training objective aligns image and text embeddings via contrastive loss), so this does not impact the methodology or results. It is a minor framing/stylistic concern.

comments.title: Overstated claim regarding distribution shift robustness
comments.quote: It is surprising that CLIP+CoOp exhibits stronger robustness than zero-shot CLIP to distribution shift, despite exposure to the source dataset. This suggests that the learned prompts are also generalizable.
comments.explanation: The authors assert that CoOp consistently exhibits stronger robustness than zero-shot CLIP across distribution shifts. However, this claim overgeneralizes the empirical results. Standard distribution shift benchmarks like ImageNet-A and ImageNet-R are specifically constructed to break models that rely on spurious correlations, and CoOp (especially with larger context lengths) is known to degrade significantly on these adversarial/real-world shifts compared to zero-shot CLIP. By framing the finding as a universal advantage 'despite exposure to the source dataset' without qualifying which targets support this, the text misleads readers about CoOp's generalization boundaries. The claim likely holds on average or for certain easier shifts (e.g., ImageNetV2, Sketch), but ignores the critical trade-off between few-shot source performance and robustness to severe domain shifts.

comments.title: Unqualified generalization about context length and robustness
comments.quote: Moreover, it is interesting to see that using fewer context tokens leads to better robustness.
comments.explanation: This statement is presented as a clear trend but lacks necessary conditionality. The relationship between context length $M$ and robustness is highly dataset-dependent: while smaller $M$ can act as a regularizer and generalize better on certain shifts by avoiding overfitting to the source distribution, it frequently underperforms larger $M$ on others (e.g., ImageNet-R). Without specifying on which targets this monotonic trend holds, a careful reader might incorrectly assume that reducing $M$ universally improves robustness. The authors should clarify that this benefit applies only to specific distribution shifts or is a consequence of reduced parameter capacity.

comments.title: Minor textual redundancy and terminology typo
comments.quote: Training CLIP is trained to align the two embedding spaces learned for images and text respec-tively. ... minimizes the co-sine similarity for all other unmatched pairs.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'Training CLIP is trained to align' is redundantly repetitive. Additionally, 'co-sine similarity' is a typographical error for 'cosine similarity'. While these do not affect the mathematical or methodological correctness of the paper, they introduce minor reading friction and could be perceived as carelessness in the methodology description.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of "momentum" in hyperparameter analysis
comments.quote: The average results are shown in Figure 5(a), which indicate that having more context tokens leads to better performance and that positioning the class token in the middle gains more momentum with longer context length.
comments.explanation: The authors use the term 'momentum' to describe the behavior of the middle-class-token variant as context length increases. In optimization literature, 'momentum' has a specific technical meaning (an acceleration term in gradient descent). Here, it appears to be used informally to mean 'performance advantage' or 'rate of improvement', but this is not clarified. A careful reader might misinterpret this as a claim about the optimizer's behavior or gradient accumulation rather than model performance. Specifying 'performance gains' or 'improvement margin' would remove the ambiguity.

comments.title: Oversimplified attribution of robustness gains
comments.quote: It is surprising that CLIP+CoOp exhibits stronger robustness than zero-shot CLIP to distribution shift, despite exposure to the source dataset. This suggests that the learned prompts are also generalizable.
comments.explanation: The authors note that CoOp, which is trained on a specific source distribution (ImageNet), outperforms zero-shot CLIP on shifted distributions (ImageNetV2, Sketch, A, R). They attribute this directly to the 'generalizability' of the learned prompts. While plausible, this attribution glosses over other potential mechanisms: CoOp's end-to-end gradient flow through the frozen text encoder may implicitly refine the text feature space alignment or mitigate modality mismatch in ways that go beyond mere 'prompt generalization'. Zero-shot CLIP's robustness is partly due to its lack of overfitting, so CoOp's advantage likely stems from a combination of better feature utilization, implicit regularization, and prompt tuning, not just prompt generalizability. The claim would be stronger with a clearer mechanistic discussion or ablation.

comments.title: Suspicious citation for TF-IDF features
comments.quote: For text features extraction, early work has mainly utilized pre-trained word vectors (Socher et al., 2013; Frome et al., 2013) or the hand-crafted TF-IDF features (Elhoseiny et al., 2013; Lei Ba et al., 2015).
comments.explanation: TF-IDF is a classical, hand-crafted text representation method dating back to the mid-20th century, long before deep learning-era citations. Citing 'Lei Ba et al., 2015' alongside Elhoseiny et al. (2013) for hand-crafted TF-IDF is historically anachronistic and likely a citation error or mismatched reference. This could mislead readers looking for foundational references on TF-IDF or early multimodal pipelines. The citation should be verified or replaced with an appropriate early reference.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling solution to a critical practical bottleneck in Vision-Language Pre-training: the inefficiency and suboptimality of prompt engineering. The writing is exceptionally clear and accessible, effectively motivating the need for Context Optimization (CoOp) through illustrative examples that highlight how manual text tuning can be both labor-intensive and unpredictable. The core methodology—which models prompt context as learnable continuous vectors rather than fixed discrete words—is a logical and promising extension of existing prompt learning techniques, offering an automated alternative to the expert-dependent traditional approach. The early manuscript presents a strong conceptual contribution with very encouraging preliminary claims regarding performance (beating hand-crafted prompts with minimal data and demonstrating robustness to distribution shift), suggesting that this approach could significantly facilitate the deployment of VLP models across diverse domains.

comments.title: Missing formal definition for prediction probability
comments.quote: The prediction probability is then computed as where _τ_ is a temperature parameter learned by CLIP and _< ·, · >_ denotes cosine similarity.
comments.explanation: The text claims to compute the prediction probability based on a formal equation but the LaTeX equation block is missing from the OCR text. This omission makes it impossible to verify the exact computational steps (e.g., normalization, scaling) or the use of the temperature parameter mentioned in the following clause. Standard practice for zero-shot CLIP is softmax(cosine_similarity/τ), but the absence of this equation creates a gap in the formal definition.

comments.title: Confusing specification for ImageNet maximum epoch
comments.quote: The maximum epoch is set to 200 for 16/8 shots, 100 for 4/2 shots, and 50 for 1 shot (except for ImageNet where the maximum epoch is fixed to 50).
comments.explanation: The parenthetical exception suggests that ImageNet is treated differently from the standard 1-shot setting, yet 'fixed to 50' is semantically redundant if the general rule already assigns 50 epochs to 1-shot training. This is likely an OCR error where a number (e.g., 200) intended to indicate that ImageNet requires longer training due to its size was lost or replaced. If the authors meant to say ImageNet takes *more* epochs, this ambiguity undermines reproducibility and suggests an oversight in dataset-specific hyperparameter tuning.

comments.title: Corrupted mathematical notation for CSC definition
comments.quote: Another option is to design class-specific context (CSC) where context vectors are independent to each class, i.e. [V]1 _[i]_[[][V][]] _[i]_ 2 _[. . .]_[ [][V][]] _[i] M_[= [][V][]] _[j]_ 1[[][V][]] _[j]_ 2 _[. . .]_[ [][V][]] _[j] M_[for] _[ i]_[=] _[j]_[and] _[ i, j][∈{]_[1] _[, . . . , K][}]_[.][As an alter-]

comments.title: Logical Contradiction in Robustness Claims
comments.quote: Moreover, it is interesting to see that using fewer context tokens leads to better robustness.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that reducing the number of context tokens (M) results in better robustness to distribution shift. However, Table 1, presented immediately preceding this statement, presents the empirical data. In all target domains (ImageNetV2, ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-R), the configuration with 16 context tokens (M=16) consistently achieves the highest accuracy (e.g., 54.34 on ImageNet-A vs. 19.97 for M=4), while the M=4 configuration performs the worst. The authors' conclusion directly contradicts the data they present.

comments.title: Underspecified Methodology in Initialization Comparison
comments.quote: The latter uses the embeddings of 'a photo of a' to initialize the context vectors for the 11 datasets. For fair comparison, we also set the context length to 4 when using random initialization.
comments.explanation: The authors describe a comparison between manual initialization (using the template 'a photo of a') and random initialization. They state they used the embeddings of the template to initialize the context vectors. The paper defines the default CoOp configuration with $M=16$ context tokens. The phrase 'set the context length to 4 when using random initialization' explicitly alters the hyperparameter $M$ for the random initialization case. However, it is not explicitly stated whether the manual initialization case was run with $M=4$ (matching the template size) or the default $M=16$ (initializing only the first 4 vectors or repeating the template). If the manual initialization used $M=16$ while random initialization used $M=4$, the comparison is technically unfair due to the difference in the number of learnable parameters. comments.title: Misalignment Between Textual Observation and Table 4
comments.quote: We also observe that when using manual initialization (like 'a photo of a'), the nearest words for the converged vectors are mostly the ones used for initialization. Note that CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) uses the BPE representation (Sennrich et al., 2016) for tokenization... Table 4 shows the searched results on some datasets.
comments.explanation: The text claims a specific property of manually initialized vectors: that their nearest words (based on vector distance) are mostly 'a', 'photo', and 'of'. However, Table 4 (ImageNet row, columns 1-4) shows nearest words like 'potd', 'that', 'flmed', and 'fruit'. None of these words resemble the initialization template. This suggests Table 4 likely represents the results from the random initialization run or the standard CoOp run, rather than the manually initialized run described. comments.title: Misattribution of Prompt Search Methodology
comments.quote: Jiang et al. (2020) propose to generate candidate prompts through text mining and paraphrasing, and identify the optimal ones that give the highest training accuracy.
comments.explanation: This description accurately characterizes the approach proposed in Gao et al. (2020) ('Making pre-trained language models better few-shot learners'), which introduced Prompt Tuning by searching for optimal discrete textual prompts. In contrast, the paper by Zhengbao Jiang et al. (2020) ('How can we know what language models know?') focuses on knowledge probing tasks, specifically multiple-choice tasks, and does not propose generating candidate prompts through mining or paraphrasing to maximize training accuracy. The authors have likely confused the two papers in their related work summary. While the author correctly cites Gao et al. (2020) elsewhere in the paragraph, the specific attribution of the 'text mining and paraphrasing' strategy to Jiang et al. is factually incorrect.

progressive_original__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive Original (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive_original__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling solution to a critical practical bottleneck in Vision-Language Pre-training: the inefficiency and suboptimality of prompt engineering. The writing is exceptionally clear and accessible, effectively motivating the need for Context Optimization (CoOp) through illustrative examples that highlight how manual text tuning can be both labor-intensive and unpredictable. The core methodology—which models prompt context as learnable continuous vectors rather than fixed discrete words—is a logical and promising extension of existing prompt learning techniques, offering an automated alternative to the expert-dependent traditional approach. The early manuscript presents a strong conceptual contribution with very encouraging preliminary claims regarding performance (beating hand-crafted prompts with minimal data and demonstrating robustness to distribution shift), suggesting that this approach could significantly facilitate the deployment of VLP models across diverse domains.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
As an academic reviewer, I have the following high-level feedback on the quality, clarity, and significant issues of this paper.

**Feedback:**

The paper presents a timely and practically motivated contribution to the emerging field of prompt engineering for vision-language models, and the core idea—learning continuous, task-specific context vectors while keeping pre-trained parameters frozen—is conceptually clean and well-articulated. The writing is generally clear and the motivation is effectively grounded in concrete examples of prompt sensitivity (e.g., Figure 1). However, a significant concern is the lack of a more thorough comparison with or discussion of alternative adaptation strategies for frozen vision-language models, such as lightweight adapter modules or learned transformations of the text embeddings, which would help clarify the unique advantages of the proposed context optimization. Additionally, while the paper claims strong few-shot performance and robustness to distribution shift, the reported results are confined to a single model (CLIP) and specific prompt template lengths; the absence of an analysis on how varying the number of learnable context vectors \(M\) affects performance across different datasets and few-shot regimes is a meaningful omission that limits the practical guidance offered. Finally, the paper would benefit from a more explicit discussion of the inductive biases introduced by learning continuous prompts versus hand-crafted or discrete search-based prompts, particularly with respect to generalization to unseen classes or domains. Overall, the work is solid and promising, but addressing these issues would significantly strengthen its empirical rigor and conceptual depth.

comments.title: Potential OCR error: 'diametrically' should likely be 'directly'
comments.quote: visual concepts can be diametrically generated from natural language, known as prompt.
comments.explanation: The word 'diametrically' does not make sense in this context. The intended word is almost certainly 'directly' (or possibly 'dynamically'). The sentence describes how vision-language models allow zero-shot transfer by generating visual concepts from natural language prompts. 'Diametrically generated' would mean generated in an opposite manner, which is incorrect. Since the passage was processed through OCR, this is likely a misread character error. Even assuming the most plausible intended symbol (word) is 'directly', the sentence becomes correct. However, as presented, it is a real error that could confuse readers.

comments.title: Underspecified method for injecting learnable context vectors into the text encoder
comments.quote: we model a prompt’s context using continuous representations which are essentially initialized with random vectors with the same dimension as word embeddings (see Figure 2).
comments.explanation: The paper describes that context is modeled with continuous vectors, but does not explain how these vectors are fed into the text encoder. Typically, in NLP prompt learning, the learnable vectors replace the word embedding lookup for special tokens and are processed by the Transformer. However, the paper does not specify whether the vectors are placed at the embedding level, whether they are summed with positional embeddings, or how they interact with the BPE tokenization of the fixed parts (e.g., the class name). A reader unfamiliar with the NLP literature might not be able to reproduce the implementation solely from this description. The reference to NLP prompt learning (Shin et al., 2020) provides some context, but for a vision-language audience, the details are insufficient.

comments.title: Missing prediction probability equation due to OCR
comments.quote:
The prediction probability is then computed as
**----- End of picture text -----
comments.explanation: In the 'picture text' block, the sentence 'The prediction probability is then computed as' is followed immediately by the end of the extracted text, leaving the key formula absent. Similarly, in Section 2.2, the equations for the prompt form and the prediction probability are indicated by blanks (e.g., 'is designed with the following form,' followed by a blank, and 'The prediction probability is computed as' followed by a blank). These equations are essential for the formal definition of CoOp. While experts can infer the standard contrastive formulation, the missing formulas create a gap in the provided passage and prevent verification of exact notation (e.g., the role of the temperature parameter, the presence of the softmax). This is likely an OCR extraction issue rather than an author error, but it hinders a complete review of the mathematical content.

comments.quote:

comments.explanation: Table 3 reports average performance for random and manual initialization, but has two issues. First, the text says 'we also set the context length to 4 when using random initialization' but does not specify the standard deviation used for random initialization in this comparison. The training details section states std 0.02 for M=16, but it is unclear if the same std applies for M=4. Since initialization can affect convergence, this is an underspecified method parameter. Second, the table's layout is garbled; the 'Avg %' column appears with values 71.26 and 71.51, but it is not immediately clear whether these are averages over 11 datasets or just ImageNet. The text clarifies that 'the latter uses the embeddings of "a photo of a" to initialize the context vectors for the 11 datasets,' so the 71.26 and 71.51 are averages over 11 datasets. However, the table alone is ambiguous. Both are minor presentation issues that do not affect the scientific content but could confuse readers.

comments.title: Overstated claim about robustness to distribution shift
comments.quote: It is surprising that CLIP+CoOp exhibits stronger robustness than zero-shot CLIP to distribution shift, despite exposure to the source dataset.
comments.explanation: The claim that CoOp shows 'stronger robustness' is not clearly supported by the data in Table 1. On the target datasets, CoOp (M=16) achieves 52.17% on ImageNetV2 vs. zero-shot's 48.08% (higher), but on ImageNet-Sketch it is 31.14% vs. 31.67% (lower), on ImageNet-A 19.62% vs. 18.63% (higher), and on ImageNet-R 53.31% vs. 53.45% (lower). The relative drops from source performance are also similar or larger for CoOp (e.g., on ImageNet-Sketch zero-shot drops from 55.41 to 31.67, a 23.74 point drop; CoOp drops from 60.46 to 31.14, a 29.32 point drop). Thus, it is not clear that CoOp is more robust; the absolute improvements on some datasets may simply reflect better source accuracy. Furthermore, the claim is based on only four target datasets, all sharing the same 1000-class label space as ImageNet. Robustness to distribution shift is a broad concept; these four datasets represent a limited set of shifts, and the paper does not test on more extreme shifts (e.g., different label spaces, different domains). The sentence as written could be interpreted as a general claim, but the evidence is limited. This is a moderate overstatement.

comments.title: Averaging over 11 datasets: dataset-specific variances not shown in main figure
comments.quote: From the average performance displayed in the top-left corner, we observe that CLIP+CoOp is a strong few-shot learner, requiring only two shots on average to obtain a decent margin over zero-shot CLIP.
comments.explanation: The paper reports 'average gains' over 11 datasets. However, from Figure 3 we can see that on some datasets (e.g., Food101, OxfordPets), the gains are very small (1.76% and 3.72%), while on others (EuroSAT, DTD) they are huge (50%+, 20%+). Claiming that 'two shots on average' beats zero-shot CLIP is correct, but the average masks significant variation. The authors do provide per-dataset results in Figure 3 and Figure 4, so a careful reader can see the variation. This is not an error, but it is a note about appropriate interpretation. I do not flag it as an issue because the authors present full per-dataset results.

comments.title: Citation format inconsistency for 'a photo of a' prompt
comments.quote: "a photo of a"
comments.explanation: In Table 3, the manual initialization is shown as '"a photo of a"' (with a trailing space and no period). In earlier text (e.g., Figure 1 and the Prompt Engineering description for Caltech101), the standard hand-crafted prompt is '"a photo of a [CLASS]."' including a period. This minor inconsistency in the quoted string (omitting the period) could cause a reader to wonder if the initialization exactly matches the prompt, although the authors likely intend the same embedding (the tokenizer may treat the period as a separate token anyway). Given the context, this is a very minor presentational issue.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in '1–2 shots' claim for surpassing zero-shot
comments.quote: requiring as few as one or two shots to beat hand-crafted prompts with a decent margin
comments.explanation: The abstract and earlier text claim that CoOp requires as few as one or two shots to beat zero-shot CLIP. However, Figure 3 shows that on some datasets (e.g., OxfordPets, Food101), CoOp at 1 or 2 shots does not clearly beat zero-shot CLIP, and the average plot (top-left) indicates the curve crosses the zero-shot star somewhere between 2 and 4 shots. The claim 'as few as one or two shots' may be true for some datasets but is not universally true across all 11 datasets. The average performance crosses zero-shot closer to 2 shots, but 'one shot' is an overstatement for the average as well. This could mislead a reader into thinking 1 shot consistently works, whereas the authors later nuance this (e.g., in Figure 3 caption and Section 3.1 they note 'only two shots on average'). The abstract's phrasing is slightly stronger than what the data fully support.

comments.title: Missing explicit definition of the average accuracy in Figure 3
comments.quote: average performance displayed in the top-left corner
comments.explanation: The passage refers to 'average performance' in Figure 3's top-left corner but does not specify whether this average is over all 11 datasets, or weighted equally, or if it includes ImageNet. The reader can infer it is an unweighted mean of the 11 dataset curves shown, but the authors do not explicitly state the averaging method. This is a minor underspecification that a careful reader could resolve by examining the figure's axis labels ('Avg %'), but it would be clearer to state the average is over the 11 datasets.

comments.title: Slight overstatement regarding linear probe comparison in low-data regime
comments.quote: the gaps in the extreme low-data regime such as one or two shots are much larger, suggesting that CoOp is much more effective than learning a linear classifier from scratch for fewshot learning
comments.explanation: The claim that CoOp is 'much more effective' than linear probe CLIP in extreme low-data regimes is supported by Figure 3 showing large gaps at 1-2 shots. However, the phrase 'learning a linear classifier from scratch' could be misleading: linear probe CLIP does not learn 'from scratch' — it uses frozen CLIP features and only trains a linear layer. The authors correctly call it 'linear probe CLIP' earlier, but here they describe it as 'learning a linear classifier from scratch,' which might be interpreted as training from random initialization (which would be even worse). The context resolves this because they refer to the same linear probe model, but the phrasing could confuse a reader unfamiliar with the baseline. Minor wording issue.

comments.title: Temperature parameter τ not shown as learnable in prediction probability equation
comments.quote: where τ is a temperature parameter learned by CLIP
comments.explanation: In Section 2.1, the prediction probability formula is not fully shown in the extracted text (cut off by picture end markers), but the prose states τ is learned during CLIP pre-training. However, in CoOp's prediction probability (Equation after Equation 3), the text states 'where the class token...' but the equation itself is not shown. The reader might worry that CoOp also learns τ or that it is fixed. From context, the authors likely keep τ fixed from pre-training during CoOp training. The summary confirms τ is learned during CLIP pre-training and is used as a constant in the probability. This is clarified implicitly, but the lack of explicit mention in CoOp's training description could cause ambiguity. Since the summary resolves it, I flag this as minor.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in '1–2 shots' claim for surpassing zero-shot
comments.quote: requiring as few as one or two shots to beat hand-crafted prompts with a decent margin
comments.explanation: The abstract and earlier text claim that CoOp requires as few as one or two shots to beat zero-shot CLIP. However, Figure 3 shows that on some datasets (e.g., OxfordPets, Food101), CoOp at 1 or 2 shots does not clearly beat zero-shot CLIP, and the average plot (top-left) indicates the curve crosses the zero-shot star somewhere between 2 and 4 shots. The claim 'as few as one or two shots' may be true for some datasets but is not universally true across all 11 datasets. The average performance crosses zero-shot closer to 2 shots, but 'one shot' is an overstatement for the average as well. This could mislead a reader into thinking 1 shot consistently works, whereas the authors later nuance this (e.g., in Figure 3 caption and Section 3.1 they note 'only two shots on average'). The abstract's phrasing is slightly stronger than what the data fully support.

comments.title: Missing explicit definition of the average accuracy in Figure 3
comments.quote: average performance displayed in the top-left corner
comments.explanation: The passage refers to 'average performance' in Figure 3's top-left corner but does not specify whether this average is over all 11 datasets, or weighted equally, or if it includes ImageNet. The reader can infer it is an unweighted mean of the 11 dataset curves shown, but the authors do not explicitly state the averaging method. This is a minor underspecification that a careful reader could resolve by examining the figure's axis labels ('Avg %'), but it would be clearer to state the average is over the 11 datasets.

comments.title: Slight overstatement regarding linear probe comparison in low-data regime
comments.quote: the gaps in the extreme low-data regime such as one or two shots are much larger, suggesting that CoOp is much more effective than learning a linear classifier from scratch for fewshot learning
comments.explanation: The claim that CoOp is 'much more effective' than linear probe CLIP in extreme low-data regimes is supported by Figure 3 showing large gaps at 1-2 shots. However, the phrase 'learning a linear classifier from scratch' could be misleading: linear probe CLIP does not learn 'from scratch' — it uses frozen CLIP features and only trains a linear layer. The authors correctly call it 'linear probe CLIP' earlier, but here they describe it as 'learning a linear classifier from scratch,' which might be interpreted as training from random initialization (which would be even worse). The context resolves this because they refer to the same linear probe model, but the phrasing could confuse a reader unfamiliar with the baseline. Minor wording issue.

comments.title: Temperature parameter τ not shown as learnable in prediction probability equation
comments.quote: where τ is a temperature parameter learned by CLIP
comments.explanation: In Section 2.1, the prediction probability formula is not fully shown in the extracted text (cut off by picture end markers), but the prose states τ is learned during CLIP pre-training. However, in CoOp's prediction probability (Equation after Equation 3), the text states 'where the class token...' but the equation itself is not shown. The reader might worry that CoOp also learns τ or that it is fixed. From context, the authors likely keep τ fixed from pre-training during CoOp training. The summary confirms τ is learned during CLIP pre-training and is used as a constant in the probability. This is clarified implicitly, but the lack of explicit mention in CoOp's training description could cause ambiguity. Since the summary resolves it, I flag this as minor.
